title: Devilled Kidneys
subtitle: Lamb's kidneys in a hot mustard and Worcestershire sauce, served on buttered toast
excerpt: Devilled kidneys is a Victorian breakfast dish that has outlasted most of its contemporaries. The kidneys cook in under 5 minutes once the pan is hot; the sauce is built in the same pan from Worcestershire sauce, mustard, cayenne, and a splash of stock. The whole dish is assembled in 15 minutes.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: devilled kidneys entry. The devilled preparation for kidneys is documented across Victorian breakfast cookbooks and was a standard item on the Victorian and Edwardian country house breakfast sideboard.

recipe.temperatureNote: Hob only; no oven temperature required.

glossaryTerms.definition: A culinary term for a preparation seasoned aggressively with hot spices, mustard, and Worcestershire sauce. A devilled dish uses all three together: the heat from cayenne, the sharpness of mustard powder, and the deep savouriness of Worcestershire. The term dates from 18th-century British cookery.

content.text: Devilled kidneys was a standard item on the Victorian breakfast sideboard and deserves its place in the modern kitchen. The kidneys must be properly prepared (white core removed, halved, and dried) and cooked fast in a very hot pan. The sauce is assembled in minutes from ingredients already in the cupboard. Overcooking is the only real way to go wrong.
Prepare the {{lamb-kidneys}} kidneys: cut each in half lengthways and remove the white core with kitchen scissors or a sharp knife. Pat dry with kitchen paper. Season well.

Heat the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a wide frying pan over a high heat until foaming. Add the kidneys cut-side down and cook for 2 minutes until browned. Turn and cook for 1 to 2 minutes more; the kidneys should be browned on the outside and just cooked through, with no pink remaining. Remove to a warm plate.
Reduce the heat to medium. Add the Worcestershire sauce, mustard powder, cayenne, and {{stock-beef}} of beef stock to the pan and stir vigorously to make the
sauce. Simmer for 2 minutes until reduced slightly. Stir in the Dijon mustard and season.
Return the kidneys to the pan and coat them in the sauce. Serve immediately on buttered toast, scattered with chopped parsley.

items.cause: Overcooked. Lamb's kidneys tighten and harden within seconds of being done.
items.fix: The kidneys need 2 minutes per side at most. They are done when just cooked through with no pink. Remove from the pan the moment they are ready and return only to coat with the sauce.

items.cause: Kidneys were old or not prepared properly.
items.fix: Use kidneys as fresh as possible. Soaking in cold salted water for 30 minutes before cooking reduces any residual ammonia smell. Trimming the core completely is also essential.

items.cause: Worcestershire sauce dominates without the stock to dilute and round it.
items.fix: Add the stock and simmer for 2 full minutes before returning the kidneys. If the sauce is still thin, add a small knob of cold butter off the heat and swirl to emulsify.

content.text: Devilled kidneys was a standard item on the Victorian and Edwardian breakfast sideboard, in country houses where offal was served as a delicacy. The Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton's version uses cayenne, mustard, and Worcestershire, the same combination used across devilled preparations of the period. The dish fell out of fashion as offal declined after the mid-20th century, but it has never fully left British menus. It is fast, cheap, and done properly, one of the better pub starters available.
